query: Angular custom two-way binding Input Output xChange interview question

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No single-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this exact Angular prompt; the available Angular basics coverage is broader than the question-specific comparison needed here.

competitors.label: Angular Two-Way Binding Guide
competitors.selectionReason: The official two-way binding guide is the canonical reference for banana-in-a-box syntax, model inputs, and the binding contract.
competitors.winReason: Angular.dev is authoritative on the syntax, but our page is more interview-useful because it now pairs the exact alias contract with the local draft sync bug and the ControlValueAccessor boundary.
theirStrengths: It authoritatively explains two-way binding syntax and shows the built-in and component-level patterns.
theirGaps: It is less tightly centered on the custom Input/Output naming contract and the failure modes that break [(x)] in interviews.

actionableExamples: Broken alias pair: Angular expands [(checked)] to [checked] + (checkedChange)
followUpConfusion: If you keep a local draft, sync it from the input and emit the next value instead of letting two sources of truth drift apart.
It stops being enough when the component must behave like a real Angular form control

realWorldUseCases: The official guide explains two-way binding well, so the practical framing stays close.
actionableExamples: Angular.dev includes concrete examples, but our page now shows the broken alias pair, the fixed alias pair, and the local draft sync pattern in one compact worked example.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about two sources of truth drifting apart and about when ControlValueAccessor is the better abstraction.

realWorldUseCases: Keep one concrete case where plain Input plus Output is enough and a real Angular Forms integration would be overkill.

competitors.label: Telerik Two-Way Data Binding Guide
competitors.selectionReason: This tutorial is a direct competitor because it focuses on banana-in-a-box syntax and the mechanics of two-way updates.
competitors.winReason: Telerik explains banana-in-a-box well, but our page is more specific about the alias mismatch bug, two-sources-of-truth drift, and when custom binding should give way to ControlValueAccessor.
theirStrengths: It is tightly focused on the same binding syntax and gives straightforward examples.
theirGaps: It is broader on general two-way binding and less specific on custom component naming traps.

actionableExamples: Broken alias pair: Angular expands [(checked)] to [checked] + (checkedChange)
followUpConfusion: If you keep a local draft, sync it from the input and emit the next value instead of letting two sources of truth drift apart.
That is the point where ControlValueAccessor becomes the right abstraction.

realWorldUseCases: Telerik stays directly on the same mechanism, so the practical use-case framing is close.
actionableExamples: Telerik explains the syntax cleanly, but our page now gives the more interview-specific broken-versus-fixed alias example and the shadow-state sync pattern.
followUpConfusion: Our page is now more explicit about why mutating the input is not enough, how local draft state drifts, and when CVA is the right next step.

realWorldUseCases: Keep the answer centered on custom component APIs rather than letting it drift into generic ngModel syntax.

competitors.label: Swiftorial Two Way Data Binding
competitors.selectionReason: This is a direct tutorial competitor that covers banana-in-a-box and even includes a custom Input plus valueChange example.
competitors.winReason: Swiftorial has a direct custom-binding example, but our page is stronger on the failure modes interviewers usually probe next: alias mismatch, local shadow-state drift, and the Angular Forms boundary.
theirStrengths: It directly covers two-way binding and includes a custom child-component example.
theirGaps: It is less explicit about aliasing rules, failure modes, and when to switch to ControlValueAccessor for real form controls.

realWorldUseCases: [(x)] works only if Angular can find:
followUpConfusion: If you keep a local draft, sync it from the input and emit the next value instead of letting two sources of truth drift apart.
It stops being enough when the component must behave like a real Angular form control

realWorldUseCases: Swiftorial covers custom two-way binding directly, but our page is more interview-shaped on the exact contract and what breaks it.
